summary: Shared client contracts are useful, but shared persistent embedding storage is not yet earned. Rerank provider infrastructure is the better extraction target because memory already has a generic Voyage/Cohere/local cross-encoder layer with caching, caps, circuit breaker fallback, and score-origin metadata.

label: memory embedding-cache semantics are portable, but shared storage would couple TypeScript memory DB lifecycle to Python CocoIndex index lifecycle before measured hit-rate overlap exists
loc_estimate: ~80-140 prod for interface/memory adapter; ~120-220 more for Coco adapter

label: Voyage Code 3 and Voyage 4 may both be 1024-dimensional, but model_id remains a hard cache dimension and cross-domain duplicate content is likely low
loc_estimate: ~0 now; ~40-70 telemetry LOC for overlap audit

label: cross-encoder.ts is already provider-generic enough to become a shared RerankClient consumed by memory and CocoIndex candidate adapters

shared indexing pipelines would leak abstractions: CocoIndex owns language-aware code chunking and code_chunks_vec, while memory owns tiered spec-doc retrieval, MMR over vec_memories, and MPAB reassembly
terminal synthesis should prioritize client extraction only when tied to a concrete consumer; otherwise embed-cache sharing should remain telemetry-only
loc_estimate: ~220-380 prod plus ~120-220 tests for both clients/adapters

cross_cutting_recommendation: Keep RQ-A5 graph fusion and RQ-B5 shared infra as separate packets. Extract a shared RerankClient only when a CocoIndex consumer is ready; defer shared embedding storage pending overlap telemetry.
